agenda_pressure: Prefer exact equivalences and recursive normal forms over nearby corollaries.
Reward proof-search results only when they expose canonical structure, not just bounded success.
Use counterexamples to isolate surviving criteria or obstructions rather than accumulate negatives.
Prefer fragment theorems that sharpen structural taxonomy or language correspondence.
current_bottlenecks: One-step decomposition theorems have not yet been iterated into canonical finite objects.
Atomic proof search has bounded completeness but not exact candidate-level or tree-level correspondence.
The all-atomic counterexample has not yet been converted into a final positive boundary theorem.
Fragment classifications are still disconnected from the proof-search and focusing story.
desired_summary_changes: An exact recursive focused decomposition-tree account for singleton and atomic derivations.
A height-preserving equivalence between focused search objects and `proveAux` success.
A positive surviving criterion or normal form for all-atomic antecedents after the known refutation.
A reusable iterated head-normal-form taxonomy for one-sided slash fragments with grammar-facing significance.
important_verified_counterexamples: ∀ {Γ : List Tp} {A : Tp}, (∀ x ∈ Γ, is_atom x) → Γ ⇒ A → is_atom A ∧ Γ = [A]
missing_bridges: A bridge from singleton outermost classification to finite focused decomposition trees.
A bridge from atomic candidate enumeration to exact soundness and completeness of last-step search.
A bridge from the all-atomic counterexample to a canonical derivation normal form.
A bridge from one-sided fragment normal forms to grammar or language-theoretic correspondence.

next_direction.guidance: Prioritize problems that turn the verified one-step decompositions and bounded search theorem into exact recursive focused trees or exact candidate correspondences, using fragment normal forms mainly when they instantiate that same recursive picture.
next_direction.label: exact_recursive_focusing
next_direction.rationale: The counterexamples block naive rigidity restorations, while the current positive theorems already isolate the outermost step; the next summary change therefore comes from exact recursive focusing interfaces rather than more local slash lemmas or stand-alone negatives.

overexplored_patterns: Single-step slash transport and composition lemmas.
Weaker restatements of already proved atomic-succedent or singleton-atom facts.
Additional negative singleton or atomic variants that stop short of a reusable criterion.
Fragment observations that do not reach an iterated normal form or exact search correspondence.

anti_goals: Avoid cosmetic reformulations that merely rename connectives, restate existing proofs in different notation, or package known systems without introducing new structural understanding.
Avoid ad hoc proliferation of structural rules introduced only to fit isolated computational or linguistic effects, without algebraic necessity, symmetry, or metatheoretic justification.
Avoid one-off examples, special parameters, and local variants whose only purpose is to capture a narrow phenomenon and which do not contribute to a reusable classification.
Avoid collections of negative instance results that do not culminate in a separation theorem, obstruction principle, or exact boundary statement.
canonical_targets: Determine the structural classification of Lambek extensions generated by controlled additions of exchange, weakening, contraction, and related directional principles.
Identify the exact boundary at which cut elimination, decidability, and complexity fail or change across disciplined families of Lambek extensions.
Formulate a sharp completeness and incompleteness theory for $L$ and its major extensions over semantically natural model classes.
Discover a structural account of the interface between Lambek derivations and categorial semantics that preserves the essential asymmetries of the calculus.
soft_constraints: Prefer standard Lambek notation and conservative extensions of it, so that new results remain legible as contributions to the established theory rather than as notation-driven rebrandings.
Prefer exact classifications, equivalence theorems, separation results, and boundary theorems over accumulations of examples, constructions, or case-specific derivations.
Prefer abstractions that remain valuable under future changes of method, and avoid dependence on transient computational fashions or tool-specific implementations.
Prefer structural principles that are algebraically motivated, proof-theoretically disciplined, and semantically interpretable, rather than tuned to produce isolated outcomes.
themes: Clarify the structural taxonomy of Lambek systems by identifying which extensions represent genuinely distinct proof-theoretic regimes and which amount to reducible variants.
Determine exact correspondences among structural rules, semantic model classes, and formal-language classes, so that proof theory, algebra, and generative capacity constrain one another sharply.
Identify the metatheoretic boundaries at which cut elimination, decidability, completeness, and complexity change, especially under partial or directional control of structural principles.
Formulate the syntax-semantics interface of categorial grammar in structural terms, so that semantic interpretation preserves proof-theoretic distinctions rather than collapsing them.
valued_problem_types: Clarify derivability, admissibility, reducibility, and independence relations among structural rules and rule schemata for $L$ and its principled extensions.
Determine when structural or logical rules are invertible, admissible, or normalization-preserving, and isolate the exact role these properties play in metatheoretic control.
Identify separation theorems that rigorously distinguish extensions by formulas, derivability classes, model classes, or language classes, rather than by isolated examples.
Formulate exact boundary results for cut elimination, decidability, and complexity under restricted exchange, weakening, contraction, and directional or partial variants thereof.

theory_snapshot: The verified core now has sharp outermost control at the atomic and singleton interfaces: atomic succedents admit a last-step slash decomposition, singleton derivability admits an outermost succedent classification, singleton-to-atom derivability collapses to equality, and atomic proof search is complete up to some finite height bound. The main frontier is no longer to find more one-step lemmas, but to turn these facts into exact recursive objects: finite focused decomposition trees, exact candidate completeness and soundness, and a height-sensitive equivalence with the Boolean search procedure. Verified counterexamples to general singleton rigidity and to the naive all-atomic antecedent theorem force progress toward surviving boundary criteria, canonical normal forms, and fragment taxonomies rather than more transport lemmas or extra negative variants.
